prompt: Before cloning a production instance for troubleshooting, what should a platform owner verify first?
choices: That clone profiles include data-preservation and post-clone cleanup requirements
That all users have local admin rights in the target instance
That update sets are committed in source and target before clone starts
That all integrations are permanently disabled in production
explanation: Clone profiles and related policies define what data is preserved or excluded and what automation runs after the clone, reducing operational risk.

prompt: Why are clone cleanup scripts commonly used in lower environments after a production clone?
choices: To force all cloned records into a separate domain automatically
To remove or mask sensitive data and reset environment-specific settings
To rollback all plugins installed in production
To copy update set history back to production
explanation: Post-clone scripts help sanitize data and reconfigure non-prod integrations, email, and endpoints so cloned environments are safe for testing.

prompt: A team needs to protect a small reference dataset in sub-prod during recurring clones. Which capability is most appropriate?
choices: Scheduled jobs
Domain map exclusions
Data preservation policy for clone
Clone source locking
explanation: Data preservation policies allow defined table records to be retained in the target while the broader clone refresh is applied.

prompt: What is the primary reason to suspend outbound email and external integrations in a cloned test instance?
choices: To improve indexing speed for cloned tables
To enable skipped update resolution
To avoid clone lock contention
To prevent unintended notifications or calls to production-connected systems
explanation: Cloned data can trigger business logic; disabling outbound paths prevents accidental communication with customers or third-party systems.

prompt: Which statement best describes clone governance for enterprise platform ownership?
choices: Clone scheduling should align with release windows, data sensitivity controls, and stakeholder communication
Clones should be run ad hoc by any admin when environments drift
Clone plans are needed only for production-to-production operations
Clone history is not required if backups exist
explanation: A governed clone process includes timing, approvals, risk controls, and notifications to minimize disruption and compliance issues.

prompt: In Upgrade Center, what is the main value of release readiness information before scheduling an upgrade?
choices: It automatically remediates all customizations
It provides visibility into family support timelines and upgrade planning data
It locks update set promotion during blackout periods
It exports a full CMDB dependency graph
explanation: Upgrade Center centralizes lifecycle and readiness details so operators can plan upgrade windows and resources with less uncertainty.

prompt: Which Upgrade Center activity helps reduce surprises during production cutover?
choices: Reviewing recommended preparation tasks and upgrade insights
Disabling all plugins before review
Cloning production after every patch note is published
Running skipped update merges only in production
explanation: Preparation tasks and insights highlight prerequisite checks and potential risk areas that should be addressed before cutover.

prompt: How does Upgrade Center support operational reporting across multiple platform teams?
choices: By replacing CAB records with automated approvals
By showing upgrade status and actionable tasks in a centralized workspace
By forcing all teams to share one update set branch
By preventing plugin activation in non-prod
explanation: Operational teams can use Upgrade Center to track progress, responsibilities, and blockers in a common view.

prompt: A platform owner wants consistent execution of upgrade tasks across environments. What is the best approach?
choices: Run production first and mirror issues backward to sub-prod
Use Upgrade Center task tracking and complete prerequisite activities in lower instances first
Skip non-prod validation to reduce lead time
Perform family upgrades only when critical defects occur
explanation: Following tracked tasks in lower environments first improves repeatability and lowers risk before production execution.

prompt: Which outcome indicates Upgrade Center is being used effectively from an operations perspective?
choices: Unplanned production upgrades with minimal documentation
Upgrade notes captured only in personal spreadsheets
Dependency tracking moved outside the platform
Clear progress visibility, assigned owners, and reduced upgrade execution variance
explanation: Effective use is demonstrated by transparent ownership, predictable execution, and fewer last-minute unknowns.

prompt: In a domain-separated enterprise instance, what does domain separation primarily provide?
choices: Data and process isolation for distinct business entities within one platform
A mandatory requirement for all scoped applications
Automatic conversion of global ACLs to scoped ACLs
A replacement for role-based access controls
explanation: Domain separation enables segmented data and process control so business units or customers can coexist on one instance safely.

prompt: When designing enterprise domain hierarchies, which principle is most important?
choices: Mirror every org chart level exactly, regardless of data access needs
Model domains around security, data-sharing, and operational ownership boundaries
Keep all records in global and rely only on UI policies
Use one domain per update set
explanation: Domain models should support practical isolation and sharing requirements, not purely organizational chart complexity.

prompt: A record must be visible to users in multiple child domains. Which configuration is typically used?
choices: Set the record to private and disable ACL inheritance
Move users to global for all shared workflows
Use domain visibility/sharing rules appropriate to the hierarchy
Convert the table to a rotated log table
explanation: Domain visibility and sharing settings allow controlled cross-domain access without collapsing overall isolation.

prompt: What is a key governance risk when domain separation is enabled but role strategy is weak?
choices: Skipped updates cannot be resolved
Clone operations are blocked permanently
Subscription counts are misreported
Users may receive broad privileges that bypass intended isolation boundaries
explanation: Domain structure alone is not sufficient; role design and ACL enforcement are needed to preserve least-privilege access.

prompt: Why should enterprise teams pilot domain separation in a limited scope before broad rollout?
choices: To validate data partitioning, shared service behaviors, and admin operating model impacts
Because domain separation can be enabled only in personal developer instances
To avoid creating ACLs for domain-separated tables
Because shared applications cannot run in domain-separated instances
explanation: A pilot helps uncover design and operational gaps early, reducing enterprise rollout risk and rework.
